unitTitle: Y8 Mobile app development (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Designing a mobile app — wireframes & user-centred design
lessons.objectives:
I can describe the Health and Fitness Tracker app and who it is for
I can list features that make an app user-friendly
I can explain what user-centred design means
I can draw a wireframe for one screen
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: mobile app · target audience · user-centred design · wireframe · feedback. TA: paper + pencils (or the digital wireframe tool) ready. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud. Online-safety note: keep health talk general — never describe an individual pupil.
1. (0–5) Starter — “how can apps help health and fitness?” Pupils TICK on the starter worksheet (no writing).
   • Support: tick-box choice (app runs on a phone/tablet).  • Core: name a health app they know.  • Challenge: who is the target audience?
2. (5–15) Our app + user-friendly features (I do → we do). Introduce the Health and Fitness Tracker and its audience; pairs discuss what makes an app easy to use, then SORT “user-friendly vs not” cards on the activity worksheet. Likely error: “user-friendly = pretty” → fix-words “user-friendly = easy to USE.”
3. (15–22) User-centred design (we do). Design around the user. • Support: word-choice.  • Core: explain user-centred design.  • Challenge: name a feature that helps the user.
4. (22–45) You-do — wireframe one screen. Show the hand-drawn sketch then the example screen; pupils draw ONE screen of their app and photograph it onto the worksheet. Order the design steps on the worksheet. TA: “prompt, do not draw it for them.”
5. (45–55) Plenary — pairs swap, give one kind comment + one idea; pupils self-rate confidence and tick the 4 “I can…”.
Differentiation is on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling — nobody’s screen looks “less done”. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Introduction to online safety — encryption, passwords & privacy
lessons.objectives:
I can explain encryption, password security and data privacy
I can choose a strong password
I can match the online-safety words to their meanings
I can explain why end-to-end encryption keeps messages private
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: online safety · encryption · password security · data privacy · consent. TA: plain paper for mind maps. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud. Safeguarding-adjacent content: keep ALL examples general and cohort-level — never single out or describe a pupil’s online life.
1. (0–5) Starter — three pictures = encryption / passwords / data privacy. Pupils CHOOSE on the starter worksheet (no writing).
2. (5–25) Concepts (I do → we do). Mind map what they know; teach encryption (a locked box + key), password security (mix of letters/numbers/symbols or three random words; a DIFFERENT password per account; two-step login), data privacy + GDPR. On the activity worksheet: MATCH word↔meaning and TICK what makes a strong password. Likely error: “longer is enough” → fix-words “long AND mixed AND different.”
3. (25–35) Case study — end-to-end encryption (a messaging app); only sender + reader can read it. • Support: recognition choice.  • Core: what encryption does + why consent matters.  • Challenge: why end-to-end encryption is good, why GDPR exists.
4. (35–50) Safe vs risky online behaviour — pupils SORT cards on the worksheet; link to ethics (consent, transparency, accountability).
5. (50–55) Plenary — pupils tick the 4 “I can…”; “name one thing that makes a password strong.”
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Understanding mobile phone hardware
lessons.objectives:
I can name common hardware parts of a phone
I can say what the CPU, RAM and sensors do
I can match each sensor to its job
I can choose which hardware an app needs
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: hardware · CPU/processor · RAM/memory · sensor · GPS · accelerometer · gyroscope. TA: Handout 1 (hardware table) ready. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — name the component from photos (CPU, RAM). Pupils CHOOSE on the starter worksheet (no writing).
2. (5–18) Inside a phone (I do → we do). Overview of components; pupils LABEL the circuit board on the activity worksheet (CPU socket, RAM, ports, expansion slots, battery). Likely error: mixes CPU and RAM → fix-words “CPU thinks, RAM remembers (for now).”
3. (18–30) Sensors. GPS = location · accelerometer = movement/steps · gyroscope = rotation · proximity = near the ear. Pupils MATCH sensor↔job and SORT components into Sensors / Memory & processing / Output.
   • Support: recognition choice (what a sensor is).  • Core: what CPU and RAM do.  • Challenge: which hardware a fitness app uses; the battery limitation of GPS.
4. (30–45) You-do — complete the hardware table (Handout 1): each component + its use + whether their app needs it; photograph onto the worksheet. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (45–55) Plenary — share answers; pupils tick the 4 “I can…”; “name one sensor and what it does.”
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Online safety in app development
lessons.objectives:
I can explain what secure data handling means
I can explain why privacy and consent matter in apps
I can spot good and poor practice in handling data
I can give my view on an ethical dilemma about user data
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: secure data handling · privacy · consent · privacy policy · ethical. TA: Handout 1 (privacy policy snippets) + Handout 2 (scenarios). Read the 4 “I can…” aloud. Keep all examples general and cohort-level.
1. (0–5) Starter — recall the online-safety ideas from Lesson 2. Pupils CHOOSE on the starter worksheet. (Optional: a short online-safety clip — teacher-played hook with motion/sound, preview first.)
2. (5–20) Secure data handling + privacy & consent (I do → we do). Apps protect and encrypt data, ask permission, and say how data is used. Pairs discuss consent; SORT “good vs poor practice” on the activity worksheet. Likely error: “safe = hidden” → fix-words “safe = protected AND only used as promised.”
   • Support: recognition choice.  • Core: why consent matters; one way to keep data secure.  • Challenge: the ethical dilemmas.
3. (20–35) Privacy policies (you-do). Groups read the snippets (Handout 1), find good/poor points, and note them.
4. (35–50) Ethical dilemmas (Handout 2). No single right answer — pupils give and justify a view in writing; photograph notes onto the worksheet.
5. (50–55) Plenary — how will online safety change THEIR app design? Tick the 4 “I can…”.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: App development (part 1) — building the welcome screen in App Lab
lessons.objectives:
I can find my way around the App Lab Design view
I can add elements (button, label, image) to a screen
I can build the welcome screen of my app
I can explain what selection (if) does in an app
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: App Lab · Design view · element · button · label · welcome screen · selection (if). TA: every pupil signed in to App Lab; wireframes from Lesson 1 to hand. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — meet App Lab; what does Run do? Pupils CHOOSE on the starter worksheet.
2. (5–18) App Lab Design view (I do). Demonstrate slowly: Design tab, Toolbox, phone screen, Properties, Run. Pupils LABEL the App Lab screen on the activity worksheet. Likely error: edits in the wrong panel → fix-words “Design to add, Properties to change.”
3. (18–40) You-do — build the welcome screen. Drag a Label + Button, change text in Properties, press Run; ORDER the “add a button” steps on the worksheet; refer back to the wireframe.
   • Support: follow the ordered steps; recognition choices.  • Core: what goes on the welcome screen; a selection (if) example.  • Challenge: why follow the wireframe. TA: “prompt, do not build it for them.”
4. (40–50) Selection (we do) — “if the user taps Start, then show the next screen.” Pupils write an “if” for their app.
5. (50–55) Plenary — paste App Lab link + screenshot; tick the 4 “I can…”.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: App development (part 2) — second screen, links & testing
lessons.objectives:
I can give helpful feedback on a classmate's app
I can add a second screen to my app
I can add a button that links between two screens
I can write a simple test plan
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: peer review · second screen · button · link · onEvent · testing · test plan. TA: App Lab open; wireframes + Lesson 5 apps to hand. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — peer review. Model kind, specific feedback; pupils pick the most helpful comment on the starter worksheet, then SORT “helpful vs unhelpful” on the activity worksheet. Keep feedback about the APP, not the person.
2. (10–20) Tutorial — add a second screen (I do). Add screen2; add a button on screen1; set its onEvent to go to screen2. Pupils ORDER the steps on the worksheet. Likely error: button does nothing → fix-words “the button needs an onEvent.”
3. (20–45) You-do — add and link a second screen; use the wireframe.
   • Support: follow the ordered steps; recognition choices.  • Core: why testing matters; what a test plan is.  • Challenge: add functionality beyond navigation; write a test (do → should happen). TA: “prompt, do not build it for them.”
4. (45–52) Testing & test plans (we do) — pupils write one test on the worksheet.
5. (52–55) Plenary — paste App Lab link + screenshot; self-rate confidence; tick the 4 “I can…”.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.
